---
title: "Copyright and Fair Use"
subtitle: "Security, Privacy, and Consumer Protection"
author: "Nick Feamster · University of Chicago"
date: last-modified
---

## Why Copyright Is in This Course {.center}

> "To **promote the Progress of Science and useful Arts**..." — U.S. Constitution, Art. I, §8

Copyright is where **code, content, and AI training data** collide with the law.
The same doctrine that decided whether Google could copy Java is now deciding
whether AI companies can train on your books.

::: {.notes}
Open with the framing: copyright feels like an entertainment-industry topic, but for
this class it is a tech-policy topic. The through-line of the lecture is one idea —
*transformative use* — and one judge — Alsup — who shows up in both the Oracle API
case and the 2025 Anthropic AI case. Tell students the four-factor test is a near-
certain midterm question (per agenda).
:::

## A 2026 Vignette: The Anthropic Decision {.smaller}

::: {.vignette}
On **June 23, 2025**, Judge William Alsup (N.D. Cal.) ruled in **Bartz v. Anthropic**
that training an LLM on books is "**exceedingly transformative**" and therefore *fair
use* — but that **downloading 7+ million pirated books** to build a permanent library
was **not** fair use. In **September 2025**, Anthropic agreed to a **~$1.5 billion**
settlement, reported as the largest copyright settlement in U.S. history.
:::

Same doctrine, opposite outcomes — **how you acquire** the work matters as much as
**what you do** with it.

::: {.notes}
This is the freshest hook. Two load-bearing facts, both verified: (1) the split ruling
— training transformative/fair use, pirated-acquisition not fair use; (2) the ~$1.5B
settlement filed Sept 2025. Note the irony: Alsup is the same judge (and former
programmer) who first ruled APIs uncopyrightable in Oracle. Don't overstate — the
settlement resolved the piracy liability; the fair-use-for-training holding stands as
district-court precedent, not yet Supreme Court law.
:::

## What Copyright Protects

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Protected** — original *expression*

- Literary, musical, dramatic, artistic works
- **Software code** (since 1980)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Not protected** — the *idea*, not the expression

- Facts, concepts, ideas
- Systems, methods, processes
:::
:::

The line is **idea vs. expression**. You can't copyright *how* to sort a list —
only a particular *written* implementation of it.

::: {.notes}
Drive the idea/expression dichotomy hard — it is the root of the whole API debate.
Feist v. Rural: phone-book listings are facts, not copyrightable. Code is treated as
expression of an implementation; that is exactly what made Oracle plausible.
:::

## The Rights a Holder Controls

A copyright is really a **bundle of exclusive rights**:

1. **Reproduction** — making copies
2. **Distribution** — selling, sharing, lending
3. **Derivative works** — adaptations, sequels, translations
4. **Public performance** — e.g., a cover song at an open mic
5. **Public display**

**Duration:** life of the author **+ 70 years**. **Infringement** = exercising any of
these without permission — and at internet scale, damages reach the **billions**.

::: {.notes}
Each right can be licensed separately — central to the entertainment business. Buying a
film on iTunes licenses the *display to yourself*, not reproduction or public
performance. Scale point: record-company suits against ISPs cover millions of works;
statutory damages stack into billion-dollar jury verdicts.
:::

## Fair Use: An Affirmative Defense {.smaller}

**Fair use is not a loophole — it's a defense you raise *after* you've copied.**

Codified at **17 U.S.C. §107**. Permits limited unlicensed use for purposes like:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Criticism and commentary
- News reporting
- Teaching and research
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Scholarship
- Parody
:::
:::

Decided **case-by-case** — there are **no bright-line rules** (no "10% rule," crediting
the artist is not permission).

::: {.notes}
Emphasize "affirmative defense": you concede a copy was made, then argue it was fair.
Common student misconceptions to pre-empt: crediting ≠ permission; "I changed 10%" is
not a thing; Creative Commons is a *license*, not a fair-use exception. Examples from
agenda: showing a reading in class, photocopying excerpts (not whole books).
:::

## The Four-Factor Test {.smaller}

Courts weigh **all four together** — no single factor decides:

| # | Factor | Core question |
|---|--------|---------------|
| 1 | **Purpose and character** | Why the copy? Is it **transformative**? |
| 2 | **Nature of the work** | Creative vs. factual/functional? |
| 3 | **Amount and substantiality** | How much — and was it the "heart"? |
| 4 | **Effect on the market** | Does it substitute for the original? |

::: {.vignette}
**Midterm signal:** know all four cold, and be ready to argue *for* and *against* fair
use given an AI case study.
:::

::: {.notes}
Per the agenda this is the single highest-probability midterm item — the professor "put
real money" on a fair-use case-study question. Have them practice articulating both
sides. Read the §107 statutory text aloud if time allows.
:::

## Factor 1: Purpose and Character

**The most important factor — and it turns on *transformativeness*.**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Favors fair use**

- Adds new meaning / purpose
- Education, research, nonprofit
- Commentary, criticism, parody
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Disfavors fair use**

- Purely commercial substitution
- Verbatim copying for the same use
- Supersedes the original
:::
:::

**Music intuition:** replaying a song = not transformative; **sampling** it into a new
track (Beastie Boys) = transformative.

::: {.notes}
Transformativeness came from Campbell v. Acuff-Rose (1994, 2 Live Crew parody). Souter:
copyright's goal "is generally furthered by the creation of transformative works." This
is the factor AI cases live or die on — courts are calling LLM training transformative
because the model learns patterns to make something new, not to regurgitate the novel.
:::

## Factor 2: Nature of the Work

**What kind of work was copied?** Copyright protects *expression*, so the closer a work
is to pure fact or function, the weaker its protection.

- **Strong protection:** novels, music, film (highly creative)
- **Weaker:** news, databases (factual)
- **Weakest:** software, instructions (functional)

Verbatim copying tends to cut **against** fair use; unpublished works get **stronger**
protection.

::: {.notes}
This is the factor that helped Google: declaring code is functional, close to an
unprotectable system. Feist again — facts aren't copyrightable at all. Note: in AI
training, plaintiffs argue creative works (novels) sit at the strong end, so factor 2
leans their way even when factor 1 favors the AI company.
:::

## Factor 3: Amount and Substantiality

Two questions, not one:

- **Quantitative:** what fraction was copied?
- **Qualitative:** was it the **"heart"** of the work?

**Counterintuitive:** copying the *whole* work can still be fair use (search-engine
thumbnails). Conversely, **300 words** out of a 200,000-word memoir was *not* fair use
in *Harper & Row* — because it was the heart.

::: {.notes}
Perfect 10 v. Amazon: entire thumbnail images copied, still fair use because
transformative (search vs. aesthetic). Harper & Row: tiny excerpt, but the most
newsworthy passage. Principle: amount is judged *relative to the transformative
purpose* — copy only what you need.
:::

## Factor 4: Effect on the Market

**Does the use substitute for, or undercut the market for, the original?**

- Direct substitution (a pirated copy people use instead of buying)
- Harm to **licensing** and **derivative-work** markets
- Considers **potential future** markets, not just current losses

::: {.vignette}
This is the authors' core argument in the AI cases: *"AI generates a romance novel in
seconds — you're putting me out of business."*
:::

::: {.notes}
Sony v. Universal (Betamax, 1984): time-shifting TV didn't harm the market — fair use,
and the home-video market then exploded. In AI litigation, market harm is the authors'
strongest factor; recent rulings, though, have leaned more on transformativeness
(factor 1) than on market effect.
:::

# Case Study: Google v. Oracle {.center}

The $9 billion fight over **11,500 lines of code**.

## Google v. Oracle: The Dispute

- **Java** API created by Sun (1995); **Oracle** bought Sun in **2010**, then sued
- **Android** (2007) reused Java's **API declarations** so developers could reuse skills
- Google copied **~11,500 lines** of *declaring code* (0.4% of the API) and wrote its
  **own** implementing code
- Oracle demanded **~$9 billion**

::: {.notes}
Set the stakes. The dispute is narrow but the principle is huge: can you copyright the
*interface* that lets software interoperate? Oracle's theory would let a company own a
standard everyone else must license.
:::

## Declaring Code vs. Implementing Code {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Declaring code** — the "menu"
(names, parameters, return types)

```java
public static int max(int a, int b)
```

*This is what Google copied (~11,500 lines).*
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Implementing code** — the "recipe"
(the actual algorithm)

```java
public static int max(int a, int b) {
    return (a > b) ? a : b;
}
```

*Google wrote all of this itself.*
:::
:::

**The analogy from the briefs:** copying the **QWERTY layout** so typists can reuse
their skill — not copying anyone's prose.

::: {.notes}
Restaurant analogy for non-CS students: declaring code is the menu listing, implementing
code is the kitchen recipe. Google reused the menu so Java developers wouldn't have to
relearn everything, and wrote its own kitchen. Justice Breyer leaned on exactly this
intuition.
:::

## The Eleven-Year Journey {.smaller}

| Year | Court | Holding |
|------|-------|---------|
| 2012 | District (Alsup) | APIs **not** copyrightable |
| 2014 | Federal Circuit | APIs **are** copyrightable *(reversed)* |
| 2016 | District (jury) | Google's use **was** fair use |
| 2018 | Federal Circuit | **Not** fair use *(reversed again)* |
| **2021** | **Supreme Court** | Google's use **was** fair use, **6–2** |

The Court **assumed** APIs were copyrightable and decided the case **on fair use**.

::: {.notes}
The procedural ping-pong is the point: even expert judges disagreed four times over 11
years. Judge Alsup — a programmer — sided with Google twice. Breyer's move was to sidestep
the copyrightability question entirely and resolve it on fair use, which is why the case
is precedent about *fair use of functional code*, not about whether APIs are copyrightable.
:::

## How the Court Applied the Four Factors {.smaller}

| Factor | Court's finding for Google |
|--------|----------------------------|
| **Purpose** | **Highly transformative** — a new platform (smartphones), new developers |
| **Nature** | Declaring code is **functional**, like an organizing system |
| **Amount** | Only **0.4%**, and only what compatibility required |
| **Market** | Different markets (mobile vs. server); blocking would harm the public's interest in **progress** |

> "Google's reimplementation... was consistent with that creative progress that is the
> basic constitutional objective of copyright itself." — *Breyer, J.*

::: {.notes}
Walk each cell. The unifying thread is factor 1 driving the rest: because the use was
transformative, the amount is read generously and the market harm is discounted (Oracle's
lost license fee isn't cognizable harm if the use is transformative). Alito/Thomas dissent:
this weakens copyright's incentive to create. Worth noting the dissent for balance.
:::

# From Code to Content: Enforcement at Scale {.center}

The law is case-by-case. The internet is **500 hours of video per minute**.

## Content ID: Filtering by Fingerprint {.smaller}

1. **Rights holders upload reference files** (songs, films) — 100M+ in the database
2. **The system fingerprints** them — audio spectral hashing, video perceptual hashing
3. **Every upload is scanned** against the database
4. **The rights holder picks an action:** **block**, **monetize** (take the ad revenue),
   or **track**

The vast majority of YouTube copyright actions run through **Content ID**, not the legal
DMCA process — billions of dollars routed to rights holders.

::: {.notes}
Content ID is private ordering: YouTube built a faster, broader system than the law
requires because manual review at this scale is impossible. The trade-off: it operates
*above* the legal floor, so it sweeps in things the law would protect.
:::

## The DMCA §512 Safe Harbor {.smaller}

The legal baseline that lets platforms exist:

1. Rights holder sends a **takedown notice**
2. Platform **removes** the content (and is **shielded from liability**)
3. User can file a **counter-notice**
4. If no lawsuit in **10–14 days**, content is **restored**

**The structural problem:** the law rewards **takedown first**. The claimant effectively
acts as judge in the early rounds, and **repeat strikes** can delete a channel — a strong
**chilling effect** on legitimate fair use.

::: {.notes}
Distinguish Content ID (private, contractual) from DMCA (statutory). The safe harbor is
why YouTube isn't liable for every user upload — but the incentive structure tilts toward
over-removal. EFF calls automated filters "censorship machines." Note the power imbalance:
the rights holder reviews the creator's own dispute.
:::

## When Filters Can't Read Context {.smaller}

Automated matching **cannot evaluate fair use** — it sees a match, not a *purpose*.

**Real over-blocking patterns:**

- Film **criticism** with short clips (commentary)
- **Parody** music videos (transformative)
- **Public-domain** classical works (rights in the *recording* vs. the composition)
- A musician's **own** song (split distribution rights)
- **Government / public-domain** footage re-claimed by a broadcaster who aired it

::: {.notes}
The classic illustration: NASA's own public-domain footage getting flagged because a news
network that aired it claimed the broadcast. Keep this general rather than over-asserting a
specific date. Tom Scott's video on why fair-use filters are impossible is a good optional
assign. The deep point: fair use needs human judgment about purpose, and matching has none.
:::

## Activity: Run the Four-Factor Test {.smaller}

**Scenario.** A YouTuber posts a **15-minute film-criticism** video: a **2-minute clip**,
frame-by-frame analysis, and short comparison clips from other films.

**Your task — apply each factor:**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
1. Purpose / character?
2. Nature of the work?
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
3. Amount and substantiality?
4. Market effect?
:::
:::

**Pair-share, 5 minutes** — then: *would Content ID block it anyway?*

::: {.notes}
Expected: (1) transformative criticism — favors fair use; (2) creative film but published —
roughly neutral; (3) ~2 min of ~100 min, fair *unless* it's the "heart"; (4) unlikely to
substitute, may even promote the film — favors fair use. Likely fair use. Punchline: a
filter would probably block it anyway, because it can't see the commentary. That gap *is*
the policy problem.
:::

## Copyright Meets AI Training {.smaller}

The Oracle question — *can you copy functional material for a transformative new
purpose?* — is now the **AI training** question.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**AI companies argue**

- Training is **transformative** (learning patterns, not copying)
- Promotes progress, like Oracle
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Rights holders argue**

- **Memorization** can reproduce works
- **Market substitution** + lost licensing
:::
:::

**2025 reality (Bartz v. Anthropic):** training on books = **fair use**; building a library
from **pirated** copies = **not** fair use. Meta won a parallel fair-use ruling days later
(June 2025). Expect the Supreme Court eventually.

::: {.notes}
This is the bridge to the AI-and-privacy/AI-copyright material and a near-certain midterm
theme. The nuance students should carry: the *training* fight is bending toward
transformative/fair use, but *how the data was obtained* is a separate, live exposure —
which is what the ~$1.5B Anthropic settlement actually resolved. Don't claim the Supreme
Court has ruled; it hasn't.
:::

## Key Takeaways

1. Copyright protects **expression**, not ideas — and serves a **constitutional** goal
2. **Fair use** is a four-factor, case-by-case **defense** — no bright lines
3. **Transformativeness (Factor 1)** drives modern outcomes
4. **Google v. Oracle:** transformative use can justify copying **functional** code
5. **Content ID / DMCA** scale enforcement but **can't judge fair use** → over-blocking
6. **AI training** is the new Oracle — and *how you got the data* is its own question

::: {.notes}
Close by returning to Alsup: the same judge and the same transformative-use idea connect a
2010 API fight to a 2025 AI fight. Tell students the four factors and the for/against AI
case-study drill are the things to over-prepare for the midterm.
:::

# Questions? {.center}

Next: **AI and Copyright** — training data, memorization, and the active litigation.
